content_en:
Al-Adab al-Mufrad attracted significant scholarly attention from early times, generating a tradition of commentary (sharh) that continues into the modern era. Understanding the major commentaries helps readers access the full depth of the collection's teachings.

Among classical works, Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani discussed many of the hadiths in Al-Adab al-Mufrad across his broader scholarship, particularly in Fath al-Bari, his monumental commentary on Sahih al-Bukhari. When treating ethical topics in the Sahih, Ibn Hajar frequently cross-referenced the Adab al-Mufrad, providing authentication analysis and legal derivations. His discussions remain essential for serious study of the collection.

The most dedicated classical engagement with the collection came through the sharh tradition. Later scholars produced specialized commentaries addressing the meaning of individual hadiths, resolving apparent contradictions between them, and drawing out legal and ethical implications. These commentaries examine al-Bukhari's chapter headings as an expression of his interpretive judgment, following the methodology of Sahih commentary in treating the headings themselves as implicit legal statements.

In the modern period, the most significant contribution to Al-Adab al-Mufrad scholarship was Shaykh Nasir ad-Din al-Albani's graded edition (Sahih al-Adab al-Mufrad and Da'if al-Adab al-Mufrad), which separated the hadiths by authentication grade. This work, while useful as a reference, has been critiqued by some scholars who argue that al-Albani's gradings occasionally diverge from those of classical hadith masters and that the practice of publishing only authenticated portions disrupts the contextual reading of the full collection.

Contemporary scholars have produced Arabic sharh works offering chapter-by-chapter commentary, tracing the chain of transmission for each hadith and explaining its application to modern social situations. Translated editions with explanatory notes have appeared in English, Urdu, and Indonesian, making the collection accessible to non-Arabic-reading audiences.

The collection is also studied through the lens of comparative ethics — examining how al-Bukhari's selection of hadiths reflects an integrated vision of social virtue that bridges individual spiritual refinement (tazkiyat an-nafs) and communal ethical responsibility. This integrative reading has made it valuable for scholars working on Islamic approaches to civil society, professional ethics, and interfaith relations.
